{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|甲山寺|version=104504390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

 (821), the imperial court awarded 20,000 sen in recognition of his achievements, a portion of which was used to construct the temple, marking its founding.

During the battles of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} period, only the principal image and a few temple treasures survived. The "Shikoku Henrei Reishōki" (published in 1689) records that "even though it was once a grand temple, it had fallen into ruin—as is the fate of many things." However, the main hall was rebuilt in the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era}} (1730), and the Daisidō (Great Master Hall) was rebuilt in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era}} (1742). An illustration in the "Shikoku Henrei Meisho Zukai" (published in 1800) shows a form close to the present one.

On {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)}} (elevation 87.2 m), it is said that during the Muromachi period an outpost of {{ill|Amagiri Castle|en|Amagiri Castle|WD=Q11443762|lt=Amagiri Castle}} once stood, evidenced by a stone stage on the summit and the presence of a commemorative stone dedicated to Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jimmu}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmyō}}. In addition, along the mountain path from the temple to the summit, stone Buddhas—dating from the late Edo period and part of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}—are arrayed.<ref>According to local signage.</ref>

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Sect Zentsuji School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Taho-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|WD=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]
== Kōyama-ji ==
; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}

{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|甲山寺|version=104504390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

 (821), the imperial court awarded 20,000 sen in recognition of his achievements, a portion of which was used to construct the temple, marking its founding.

During the battles of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} period, only the principal image and a few temple treasures survived. The "Shikoku Henrei Reishōki" (published in 1689) records that "even though it was once a grand temple, it had fallen into ruin—as is the fate of many things." However, the main hall was rebuilt in the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era}} (1730), and the Daisidō (Great Master Hall) was rebuilt in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era}} (1742). An illustration in the "Shikoku Henrei Meisho Zukai" (published in 1800) shows a form close to the present one.

On {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)}} (elevation 87.2 m), it is said that during the Muromachi period an outpost of {{ill|Amagiri Castle|en|Amagiri Castle|WD=Q11443762|lt=Amagiri Castle}} once stood, evidenced by a stone stage on the summit and the presence of a commemorative stone dedicated to Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jimmu}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmyō}}. In addition, along the mountain path from the temple to the summit, stone Buddhas—dating from the late Edo period and part of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}—are arrayed.<ref>According to local signage.</ref>

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Sect Zentsuji School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Taho-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|WD=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]

* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Sect Zentsuji School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Taho-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|WD=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]
== Kōyama-ji ==
; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}
: 73 {{ill|Shusshaka-ji|ja|出釈迦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusshaka-ji|qq=}} --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-* '''74 Kōyama-ji''' --{{small|(1.6 km)}}-- 75 {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsū-ji}}

{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|甲山寺|version=104504390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

 (821), the imperial court awarded 20,000 sen in recognition of his achievements, a portion of which was used to construct the temple, marking its founding.

During the battles of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} period, only the principal image and a few temple treasures survived. The "Shikoku Henrei Reishōki" (published in 1689) records that "even though it was once a grand temple, it had fallen into ruin—as is the fate of many things." However, the main hall was rebuilt in the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era}} (1730), and the Daisidō (Great Master Hall) was rebuilt in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era}} (1742). An illustration in the "Shikoku Henrei Meisho Zukai" (published in 1800) shows a form close to the present one.

On {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)}} (elevation 87.2 m), it is said that during the Muromachi period an outpost of {{ill|Amagiri Castle|en|Amagiri Castle|WD=Q11443762|lt=Amagiri Castle}} once stood, evidenced by a stone stage on the summit and the presence of a commemorative stone dedicated to Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jimmu}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmyō}}. In addition, along the mountain path from the temple to the summit, stone Buddhas—dating from the late Edo period and part of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}—are arrayed.<ref>According to local signage.</ref>

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Zentsuji Sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan|1=Iozen}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Q16282425|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|WD=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike|1=Manno Lake}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]
== Kōyama-ji ==
; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}
: 73 {{ill|Shusshaka-ji|ja|出釈迦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusshaka-ji|qq=}} --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-* '''74 Kōyama-ji''' --{{small|(1.6 km)}}-- 75 {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsū-ji|1=Zentsū-ji}}

{{Shikoku 88 Temples}}

{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|甲山寺|version=104504390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

 (821), the imperial court awarded 20,000 sen in recognition of his achievements, a portion of which was used to construct the temple, marking its founding.

During the battles of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} period, only the principal image and a few temple treasures survived. The "Shikoku Henrei Reishōki" (published in 1689) records that "even though it was once a grand temple, it had fallen into ruin—as is the fate of many things." However, the main hall was rebuilt in the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}} (1730), and the Daisidō (Great Master Hall) was rebuilt in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}} (1742). An illustration in the "Shikoku Henrei Meisho Zukai" (published in 1800) shows a form close to the present one.

On {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (elevation 87.2 m), it is said that during the Muromachi period an outpost of {{ill|Amagiri Castle|en|Amagiri Castle|WD=Q11443762|lt=Amagiri Castle|1=Amagiri Castle}} once stood, evidenced by a stone stage on the summit and the presence of a commemorative stone dedicated to Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmyō|1=Emperor Kōmei}}. In addition, along the mountain path from the temple to the summit, stone Buddhas—dating from the late Edo period and part of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}—are arrayed.<ref>According to local signage.</ref>

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era|1=Meiwa}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era|1=Tenmei}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era|1=Hōreki}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu|1=Candraprabha}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

* Expressway: {{ill|Takamatsu Expressway|en|Takamatsu Expressway|WD=Q7677132|lt=Takamatsu Expressway|1=Takamatsu Expressway}}, {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} (4.5 km)

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Zentsuji Sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan|1=Iozen}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Q16282425|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|WD=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike|1=Manno Lake}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]

{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|甲山寺|version=104504390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

 (821), the imperial court awarded 20,000 sen in recognition of his achievements, a portion of which was used to construct the temple, marking its founding.

During the battles of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} period, only the principal image and a few temple treasures survived. The "Shikoku Henrei Reishōki" (published in 1689) records that "even though it was once a grand temple, it had fallen into ruin—as is the fate of many things." However, the main hall was rebuilt in the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}} (1730), and the Daisidō (Great Master Hall) was rebuilt in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}} (1742). An illustration in the "Shikoku Henrei Meisho Zukai" (published in 1800) shows a form close to the present one.

On {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (elevation 87.2 m), it is said that during the Muromachi period an outpost of {{ill|Amagiri Castle|en|Amagiri Castle|WD=Q11443762|lt=Amagiri Castle|1=Amagiri Castle}} once stood, evidenced by a stone stage on the summit and the presence of a commemorative stone dedicated to Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmyō|1=Emperor Kōmei}}. In addition, along the mountain path from the temple to the summit, stone Buddhas—dating from the late Edo period and part of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}—are arrayed.<ref>According to local signage.</ref>

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era|1=Meiwa}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era|1=Tenmei}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era|1=Hōreki}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu|1=Candraprabha}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Zentsuji Sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan|1=Iozen}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Q16282425|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|WD=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike|1=Manno Lake}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]

{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|甲山寺|version=104504390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

 (821), the imperial court awarded 20,000 sen in recognition of his achievements, a portion of which was used to construct the temple, marking its founding.

During the battles of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} period, only the principal image and a few temple treasures survived. The "Shikoku Henrei Reishōki" (published in 1689) records that "even though it was once a grand temple, it had fallen into ruin—as is the fate of many things." However, the main hall was rebuilt in the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}} (1730), and the Daisidō (Great Master Hall) was rebuilt in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}} (1742). An illustration in the "Shikoku Henrei Meisho Zukai" (published in 1800) shows a form close to the present one.

On {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (elevation 87.2 m), it is said that during the Muromachi period an outpost of {{ill|Amagiri Castle|en|Amagiri Castle|WD=Q11443762|lt=Amagiri Castle|1=Amagiri Castle}} once stood, evidenced by a stone stage on the summit and the presence of a commemorative stone dedicated to Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmyō|1=Emperor Kōmei}}. In addition, along the mountain path from the temple to the summit, stone Buddhas—dating from the late Edo period and part of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}—are arrayed.<ref>According to local signage.</ref>

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era|1=Meiwa}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era|1=Tenmei}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era|1=Hōreki}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu|1=Candraprabha}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan|1=Iozen}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Q16282425|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} pilgrimage. * The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).

{{Shikoku 88 Temples}}

{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|甲山寺|version=104504390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

 (821), the imperial court awarded 20,000 sen in recognition of his achievements, a portion of which was used to construct the temple, marking its founding.

During the battles of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} period, only the principal image and a few temple treasures survived. The "Shikoku Henrei Reishōki" (published in 1689) records that "even though it was once a grand temple, it had fallen into ruin—as is the fate of many things." However, the main hall was rebuilt in the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}} (1730), and the Daisidō (Great Master Hall) was rebuilt in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}} (1742). An illustration in the "Shikoku Henrei Meisho Zukai" (published in 1800) shows a form close to the present one.

On {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (elevation 87.2 m), it is said that during the Muromachi period an outpost of {{ill|Amagiri Castle|en|Amagiri Castle|WD=Q11443762|lt=Amagiri Castle|1=Amagiri Castle}} once stood, evidenced by a stone stage on the summit and the presence of a commemorative stone dedicated to Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmyō|1=Emperor Kōmei}}. In addition, along the mountain path from the temple to the summit, stone Buddhas—dating from the late Edo period and part of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}—are arrayed.<ref>According to local signage.</ref>

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era|1=Meiwa}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era|1=Tenmei}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era|1=Hōreki}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu|1=Candraprabha}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Zentsuji Sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan|1=Iozen}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Q16282425|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|WD=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike|1=Manno Lake}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]
== Kōyama-ji ==
; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era|1=Meiwa}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era|1=Tenmei}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era|1=Hōreki}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu|1=Candraprabha}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Zentsuji Sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|WD=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan|1=Iozen}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Q16282425|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|WD=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike|1=Manno Lake}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]
== Kōyama-ji ==
; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}
: 73 {{ill|Shusshaka-ji|ja|出釈迦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shusshaka-ji|qq=}} --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-* '''74 Kōyama-ji''' --{{small|(1.6 km)}}-- 75 {{ill|Zentsū-ji|en|Zentsū-ji|WD=Q787035|lt=Zentsū-ji|1=Zentsū-ji}}

{{Buddhism-stub}}

{{translated page|ja|甲山寺|version=104504390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

 (821), the imperial court awarded 20,000 sen in recognition of his achievements, a portion of which was used to construct the temple, marking its founding.

During the battles of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} period, only the principal image and a few temple treasures survived. The "Shikoku Henrei Reishōki" (published in 1689) records that "even though it was once a grand temple, it had fallen into ruin—as is the fate of many things." However, the main hall was rebuilt in the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}} (1730), and the Daisidō (Great Master Hall) was rebuilt in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}} (1742). An illustration in the "Shikoku Henrei Meisho Zukai" (published in 1800) shows a form close to the present one.

On {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|WD=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (elevation 87.2 m), it is said that during the Muromachi period an outpost of {{ill|Amagiri Castle|en|Amagiri Castle|WD=Q11443762|lt=Amagiri Castle|1=Amagiri Castle}} once stood, evidenced by a stone stage on the summit and the presence of a commemorative stone dedicated to Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmyō|1=Emperor Kōmei}}. In addition, along the mountain path from the temple to the summit, stone Buddhas—dating from the late Edo period and part of the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}—are arrayed.<ref>According to local signage.</ref>

== Temple Complex ==
{{File:Kouyamaji kyuusannmonn1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Main Gate}}
{{File:Kouyamaji bisyamongutu1.jpg|100px|thumb|Old Bishamonten Cave}}
* **Main Gate:** Fell into disrepair in 2008.
* **Middle Gate:** Rebuilt in 1769 (the 6th year of the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era|1=Meiwa}}) as the middle gate.
* **Main Hall:** Completed in 1735 (the 20th year of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}}). Visitors are required to remove their footwear to view the principal image. The principal image is a seated statue of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (a monolithic carving from Hiwamatsu with carved eyes); the attendant Buddhas include a standing statue of {{ill|Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja|日光月光菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Gekkō Bosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a standing statue of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} on the right flank, and a seated statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} on the left.
* **Daisidō (Great Master Hall):** Completed in 1742 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|WD=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō era|1=Kanpō}}). A statue of the Great Master in black robes was unveiled in 2014 and 2020.
* **Guest Hall:** Features fusuma painted with the Heart Sutra by calligrapher Kohei Okamoto.
* **Goma Hall:** Completed in 1782 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei era|1=Tenmei}}). Houses a seated statue of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} and two child attendants.
* **Tsuya Jizō:** Located to the right in front of the Daisidō; according to tradition, carrying its apron is said to bring about the birth of a child.
* **Awashima Daimyōjin Shrine (Hokora)**
* **Bell Tower:** Completed in 1752 (the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki era|1=Hōreki}}).
* **16 Rabbits:** Derived from depictions of rabbits on the accessory of the attendant Buddha {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|WD=Q2671293|lt=Gekkō Bosatsu|1=Candraprabha}} beside the principal image, rabbits are represented on the interior of the Main and Middle Gates, as well as on the roof of the tea house and in front of the chozubasho.<ref>{{Cite news |和書 |title=Welcome to Rabbit Temple – “16 Rabbits” present on the precincts, also used as talismans at Zentsūji/Koyama-ji |newspaper=Shikoku Shimbun |date=2020-04-03 |url=https://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |accessdate=2023-07-15 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200405172931/http://www.shikoku-np.co.jp/bl/digital_news/article.aspx?id=K2020040300000001300 |archivedate=2020-04-05 }}</ref>
* Proceeding through the Main Gate and past the hand-washing basin to the right, turning left to pass through the Middle Gate leads to a Nōkyō (scripture hall) on the right. A short flight of stone steps then leads to the Main Hall; to its right lies the Goma Hall, and a few steps up on the left is the Daisidō. Beyond that is the Bell Tower, and further on is the entrance to the Bishamonten Cave, within which a stone statue of Bishamonten is enshrined.
* **Lodging (Shukubō):** None.
* **Parking:** A free large parking lot is available in front of the Main Gate.
* **Suijin Shrine (Hokora):** Enshrined at the highest point of the precincts.
* **Mini Nishi-koku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage:** Markers along the walking path from the precincts to the summit.

== Cultural Properties ==
; National Historic Site
* The pilgrimage route of the {{ill|Sanuki Pilgrimage|ja|遍路道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Pilgrimage|1=henro-michi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11640512}} within the precincts of Koyama-ji – This route originally wound eastward around the northern part of Koyama, reached the old Main Gate, then crossed a stone bridge (now removed) over the Hirota River in front of Zentsūji, continuing south. It was recommended in a report on December 20, Reiwa 6.<ref>https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/documents/51893/kouyamazi_shitei.pdf</ref>

== Transportation ==
; **Rail**
* {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=Shikoku Railway Company (JR Shikoku)|1=Shikoku Railway Company}}, {{ill|Dosan Line|en|Dosan Line|WD=Q861077|lt=Tosan Line|1=Dosan Line}} – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|en|Zentsūji Station|WD=Q899921|lt=Zentsūji Station|1=Zentsūji Station}} (3.6 km)

'''Koyama-ji''' (甲山寺, こうやまじ) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|ja|真言宗善通寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Shentsū-ji Sect|1=Shingon Zentsuji Sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11583514}} located in {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|qid=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|qid=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji City|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}. It is also known by its mountain name, {{ill|Iozen|en|Iozen|qid=Q11404619|lt=Iōzan|1=Iozen}}, and by its temple name, {{ill|Taho-in|ja|多宝院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taho-in|1=Q16282425|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16282425}}. The principal image is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, and it is the 74th temple on the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|qid=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku 88 Temples|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} pilgrimage. The temple is situated at the foot of {{ill|Mount Kabuto|en|Mount Kabuto|qid=Q8524598|lt=Koyama (Kagawa)|1=Mount Kabuto}} (also called Kabutoyama).

* The mantra of the principal image is: "on korokoro sendarimātōgi sowaka" (おん ころころ せんだりまとうぎ そわか).
* The temple hymn (ご詠歌) is: "In battles where the Twelve Deities (十二神) are my allies, may my heart be like Koyama" (十二神味方に持てる戦には　おのれとこころ甲やまかな).
* The Nōkyō seal includes the temple’s principal image, the guardian deity {{ill|Tamonten|ja|多聞天堂毘沙門天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a rabbit seal (worth 200 yen).

== Overview ==
{{File:Kouyamaji20210309 1.jpg|170px|left|thumb|Koyama-ji}}
According to legend, during his prime, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} once explored this area—where he had played as a child—and encountered an old man who prophesied that a temple should be built here. Believing the old man to be an incarnation of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} who dwelled in a local cave, Kūkai enshrined Bishamonten in that cave.
Later, upon receiving an imperial edict from Emperor {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|qid=Q316654|lt=Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} to rebuild {{ill|Manno Lake|en|Manno Lake|qid=Q20983084|lt=Mannō-ike|1=Manno Lake}}, Kūkai inscribed an image of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} and conducted rituals that drew tens of thousands of people, leading to the successful Completion tier 1]]
== Kōyama-ji ==
; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|qid=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}
